query: Does Rhinolophus sinicus live in caves?

treatments.articleTitle: Recent surveys of bats (Mammalia: Chiroptera) from China. I. Rhinolophidae and Hipposideridae
treatments.articleAuthor: Zhang, Libiao, Jones, Gareth, Zhang, Jinshuo, Zhu, Guangjian & Parsons, Stuart

images.captionText: FIG. 4. Representative echolocation calls of bats in the family Hipposideridae captured from China during our surveys. See text for data on intraspecific variation. Calls are from time expanded sequences of handheld bats with FFT size 1024 Hz, Hanning window. The species are arranged according to body size from large species (left) to small (right)

treatments.articleTitle: Morphological and mitochondrial-DNA variation in Rhinolophus rouxii (Chiroptera)

treatments.articleTitle: Karyology of eight species of bats (Mammalia: Chiroptera) from Hainan Island, China
treatments.articleAuthor: Wu, Yi, Motokawa, Masaharu, Li, Yu-Chun, Harada, Masashi, Chen, Zhong & Lin, Liang-Kong

images.captionText: Figure 3 Conventional (A), G-banding (B), and C-banding karyotypes of Rhi- nolophus sinicus from Hainan Island, China. Secondary constrictions are indicated by arrows.

response.answer:
According to the text, yes, Rhinolophus sinicus is known to roost in caves. In fact, in Mussoorie, Uttar Pradesh (India), individuals of R. sinicus were found by the author roosting in a cave with R. ferrumequinum and R. affinis.

Additionally, it's mentioned that in cold regions, R. sinicus hibernates during winter, which suggests that caves may be used as hibernation sites for this species.
